content: I'll start by reading the required context files.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
85

94	## LSP MCP tools
95	
96	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
97	
98	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
99	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
100	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
101	
102	## Search protocol
103	
104	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
105	
106	1. `lean_local_search` first.
107	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
108	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
109	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
110	
111	## Tooling traps
112	
113	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
114	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

39	## Decisions identified
40	
41	### Decision 1: how to express the generic rank `m`
42	
43	- **Mathlib idiom**: `m := Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N)`.
44	  `P_d = MvPolynomial (Fin d) A` is a noetherian domain (`MvPolynomial.isDomain`, Hilbert basis), so
45	  `nonZeroDivisors P_d` localizes `N` to a module over `Localization (nonZeroDivisors P_d) = FractionRing P_d`,
46	  a finite-dimensional `FractionRing P_d`-vector space (finite, since `N` is finite over `P_d`).
47	  `Module.finrank` over a field is the right invariant; `Module.rank` (`Cardinal`-valued) is the wrong
48	  altitude — you want a `ℕ` to index `Fin m`.
49	- **Choosing `m` lifts of `N` whose images form a basis** — atoms all verified:
50	  - `Module.finBasis (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule … N) : Basis (Fin m) (FractionRing P_d) (…)`
51	    (`Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.Free`) — the field-vector-space basis indexed by `Fin (finrank)`.
52	  - `IsLocalizedModule.surj (nonZeroDivisors P_d) (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap …)` — every basis vector
53	    is `(1/s) • (image of some n ∈ N)`; `s` a unit in the field, so the **images** `{mk n_i}` are again a
54	    basis (clear denominators). (`Mathlib.Algebra.Module.LocalizedModule.Basic`.)
55	  - `LinearIndependent.restrict_scalars` (P_d ↪ FractionRing P_d injective) descends linear independence
56	    of the lifts from `FractionRing P_d` back to `P_d` — this is what makes `φ` (below) injective.
57	  - Reverse helper `Module.Basis.ofIsLocalizedModule` / `LinearIndependent.localization`
58	    (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Module`) go R→Rₛ; useful for the spanning/surjectivity half.
59	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent — Mathlib has every atom but **no single packaged "choose `m` elements
60	  whose images are a basis of the localization" lemma**. The number is idiomatic; the lift is project glue.
61	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on the number (`Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule …)`),
62	  NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL on the lift (small, all anchors present).
63	
64	### Decision 2: encoding the generic-rank SES `gf_generic_rank_ses`
65	
66	- **Mathlib idiom for the map**: `φ := Fintype.linearCombination P_d v` where `v : Fin m → N` are the
67	  lifts of Decision 1 (`Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Finsupp.LinearCombination`). Domain `(Fin m → P_d)` **is**
68	  the concrete `P_d^{⊕m}` (matches the `M'` slot of L3 exactly). Companions: `Fintype.linearCombination_apply`
69	  (`∑ i, f i • v i`), `Fintype.range_linearCombination` (`range = span (Set.range v)`).
70	  `φ` injective ⟺ `LinearIndependent P_d v` (got from Decision 1 via `restrict_scalars`).
71	- **Cokernel / torsion**: `T := N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ`; the surjection is `Submodule.mkQ (range φ)`
72	  (surjective, and `Function.Exact φ (mkQ (range φ))` holds — `range φ = ker (mkQ)`). `T` torsion encoded
73	  as `Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T` (preferred — it is the literal input of the reindex's
74	  annihilator anchor) or equivalently `Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) T)` via
75	  `LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff`. Torsion holds because the images of `v` span the localization
76	  (basis), so `φ ⊗ FractionRing P_d` is surjective, hence `T ⊗ FractionRing P_d = 0`.
77	- **Important simplification vs the directive**: the SES is constructible over `P_d` **directly — no
78	  inversion of `g ∈ A` is needed for this step**. `φ` is injective on the nose (`ker φ` is torsion in the
79	  torsion-free `P_d`-module `P_d^{⊕m}`, hence `0`), and `T` is torsion on the nose. The `g` that Nitsure
80	  mentions belongs entirely to the *reindex* (Decision 3), not the SES. So `gf_generic_rank_ses` carries
81	  no `g`. (The "denominator-clearing exactness after inverting one element" API the directive asked about
82	  is therefore unnecessary here.)
83	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL — no packaged generic-rank SES, but every atom exists and the assembly
84	  is short.
85	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (build `gf_generic_rank_ses` from the atoms above).
86	
87	### Decision 3: the torsion reindex `gf_torsion_reindex` — and the base-domain induction shape
88	
89	- **Nonzero annihilator (Mathlib ✓)**: `Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors`
90	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Module.Torsion.Basic`): a finite `Module.IsTorsion R M` over comm `R` has
91	  `(⊤.annihilator ∩ nonZeroDivisors R).Nonempty`. For the domain `P_d` this gives `0 ≠ F ∈ Ann_{P_d}(T)`.
92	- **Variable drop (Mathlib-ABSENT)**: from `0 ≠ F ∈ Ann(T)`, one wants `T` module-finite over a polynomial
93	  ring in `m' < d` variables. Cleanest route that **avoids Krull-dimension theory**: a Nagata change of
94	  variables makes `F` monic in `X_d` up to its leading coefficient `g ∈ A`; after inverting `g`,
95	  `A_g[X_1..X_d]/(F)` is module-finite (division algorithm) over `A_g[X_1..X_{d-1}] = MvPolynomial (Fin (d-1)) A_g`,
96	  and `T` (finite over `P_d/(F)`) is finite over it by `Module.Finite.trans`. So `m' = d-1 < d` — **no
97	  `ringKrullDim` needed**. Mathlib has the *field* Noether normalization `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`
98	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.NoetherNormalization`) and `ringKrullDim` theory (`Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.*`,
99	  e.g. `Polynomial.ringKrullDim_le`) but **not** the domain-level single-variable elimination with
100	  denominator clearing. This is the **same engine** the already-stubbed L4
101	  `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial` needs — build once, reuse.
102	- **CRITICAL design consequence — the induction must generalize the base domain `A`.** The reindex inverts
103	  `g ∈ A`, so `T_g` is finite over `MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g)` — base ring `A_g`, **not**
104	  `A`. The IH of `exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial` as currently written fixes `A`, so
105	  `IH m' _ T_g` does **not typecheck** (wrong base ring). This is the real cause of the iter-006
106	  "signatures depend on the generic-rank API" stall: it is not the rank API, it is that the recursion
107	  changes the base. **Fix**: state/prove the core with the motive universally quantifying the noetherian
108	  domain — `∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) […], ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Free A_f N_f`,
109	  by `Nat.strong_induction_on d` with `A` (and its three instances, and all of `N`'s `d`-dependent
110	  instances) reverted into the motive. `A` is already an explicit argument, so this is a reversion change,
111	  not a signature change to the public lemma. Apply `IH m' (hm' : m' < d) (Localization.Away g) T_g` at
112	  base `A_g` (still a noetherian domain: `IsLocalization.isDomain`/`Localization.Away` of a noeth domain).
113	- **Descent of the IH witness back to `A`**: IH gives `h ≠ 0` in `A_g` with `T_g` free over `(A_g)_h`.
114	  Since `A_g = Localization.Away g` and `h = a / g^k`, `(A_g)_h ≅ A_{g·a}` and `T_{g·a} ≅ (T_g)_h`, so the
115	  freeness descends to `f'' := g·a ∈ A`. This is the same localization-tower freeness transport as the
116	  already-proved L3b `free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul` (a thin bridge across `A → A_g → (A_g)_h`).
117	- **The `P_d^{⊕m}` end is free over `A_f` for free (Mathlib ✓)**: `MvPolynomial.instFree`
118	  (`Module.Free A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)`) + `Pi`/localization free, so the `M'` end of L3 is free over
119	  `A_{f'}` for *any* `f' ≠ 0`. (Note: the file's comment "applied coordinatewise via the d=0 leaf" is
120	  slightly off — `P_d^{⊕m}` is **not** module-finite over `A`, but it **is** free, which is all L3 needs.)
121	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (variable-drop engine + base-`A` generalization).
122	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
123	
124	## Recommended sub-lemma signatures (for the blueprint pin)
125	
126	```lean
127	/-- `gf_generic_rank_ses` — the generic-rank short exact sequence. No `g`: built over `P_d` directly. -/
128	theorem gf_generic_rank_ses
129	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
130	    (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
131	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
132	    [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
133	    ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
134	      Function.Injective φ ∧
135	      Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
136	-- witness: m = finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N);
137	--          φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, v the denominator-cleared basis lifts.
138	```
139	
140	```lean
141	/-- `gf_torsion_reindex` — re-present a finite torsion `P_d`-module over fewer variables after
142	inverting `g ∈ A`. Mirrors the existential style of the already-present L4
143	`exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`. -/
144	theorem gf_torsion_reindex
145	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
146	    (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
147	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
148	    [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
149	    (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
150	    ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
151	      (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
152	              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
153	      (_ : Module (Localization.Away g) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
154	      (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
155	              (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
156	              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
157	      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
158	        (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
159	-- can fix m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); strong induction accepts any m' < d.
160	```
161	
162	And the **core's proof must be restructured** to generalize `A`:
163	```lean
164	-- helper, then `exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial` is its specialization:
165	∀ d, ∀ (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
166	       (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
167	       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] [Module A N]
168	       [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N],
169	     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
170	-- proved by `Nat.strong_induction_on d`, A reverted into the motive.
171	```
172	
173	## Recommendation
174	Pin `m := Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d) (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N)` as the generic
175	rank — idiomatic and `ℕ`-valued. Build `gf_generic_rank_ses` (no `g`) from `Fintype.linearCombination`
176	on denominator-cleared basis lifts (`Module.finBasis` + `IsLocalizedModule.surj` +
177	`LinearIndependent.restrict_scalars`), with `T := N ⧸ range φ` and `Module.IsTorsion P_d T`. Build
178	`gf_torsion_reindex` on the L4 existential template, using `Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors`
179	for `0 ≠ F ∈ Ann(T)` and the **project-side** Nagata single-variable elimination + denominator clearing
180	(shared with L4) to land over `MvPolynomial (Fin (d-1)) (Localization.Away g)`. **The load-bearing fix is
181	structural**: generalize the base domain `A` in the `Nat.strong_induction_on`, because the reindex changes
182	the base ring to `A_g` and the IH must be available there. None of these require weakening any pinned type.

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	\end{theorem}
65	
66	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
67	% exactly this signature):
68	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
69	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
70	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
71	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
72	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
73	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
76	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
77	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
78	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
79	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
80	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
81	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
82	% there exists a finite filtration
83	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
84	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
85	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
86	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
87	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
88	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
89	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
90	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
91	\begin{proof}
92	
93	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
94	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
95	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
96	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
97	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
98	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
99	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
100	
101	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
102	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
103	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
104	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
105	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
106	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
107	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
108	  for exactly this residue.
109	
110	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
111	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
112	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
113	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
114	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
115	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
116	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
117	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
118	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
119	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
120	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
121	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
122	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
123	\end{proof}
124	
125	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
126	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
127	
128	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
129	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
130	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
131	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
132	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
133	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
134	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
135	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
136	
137	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
138	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
140	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
141	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
142	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	
147	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
148	  finitely presented, so
149	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
150	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
151	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
152	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
153	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
154	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
155	  \(f := r\).
156	\end{proof}

158	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
159	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
160	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
161	  \mathlibok
162	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
163	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
164	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
165	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
166	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
167	\end{lemma}

169	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
170	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
172	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
173	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
174	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
175	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
176	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
177	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
178	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
179	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
180	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
181	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
182	  induction).}
183	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
184	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
185	  \(A_f\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
191	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
192	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
193	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
194	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
195	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
196	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
197	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
198	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
199	\end{proof}

201	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
202	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
203	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
204	  \mathlibok
205	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
206	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
207	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
208	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
209	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
210	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
211	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
212	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
213	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
214	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
215	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
216	\end{lemma}

218	\begin{lemma}[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
219	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
221	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
222	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
223	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
224	  exact sequence
225	  \[
226	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
227	  \]
228	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
229	\end{lemma}
230	
231	\begin{proof}
232	
233	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
234	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
235	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence. Proved directly in Lean.
236	\end{proof}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
239	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
241	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
242	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
243	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
244	\end{lemma}
245	
246	\begin{proof}
247	
248	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
249	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
250	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
251	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
252	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
253	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
254	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
258	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
260	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
261	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
262	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	
267	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
268	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
269	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
273	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
275	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
276	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
278	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
279	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
280	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
281	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
282	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
283	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
284	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
285	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
286	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
288	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
289	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
290	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
291	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
296	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
298	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
299	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
300	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
301	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
302	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
303	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
304	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
305	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
306	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
307	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
308	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
309	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
310	  free over \(A_f\).
311	\end{proof}

313	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
314	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
315	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
316	  \mathlibok
317	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
318	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
319	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
320	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
321	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
322	\end{lemma}

324	\begin{lemma}
325	
326	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
327	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
329	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
330	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
331	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
332	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
333	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
334	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
336	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
338	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
339	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
340	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
341	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
342	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
343	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
344	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
345	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
346	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
347	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
348	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
349	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
350	  %         p ∈ Set.range
351	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
352	  % NOTE (encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
353	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
354	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
355	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
356	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
357	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
358	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
359	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
360	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
361	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
362	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
363	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
364	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
365	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
371	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
372	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
373	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
374	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
375	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
376	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
377	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
378	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
379	\end{proof}

381	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
382	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
383	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
384	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
385	  % NOTE: the LEAN SIGNATURE block below now matches the landed
386	  % Lean decl `exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial`, which RETAINS the binder
387	  % `(_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))` as
388	  % the 3rd existential (after `(_ : g ≠ 0)`, before `φ`) — required to type the
389	  % AlgHom target as an A_g-algebra. The MATH statement is unaffected.
390	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (landed target, AlgHom encoding). Write
391	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g,   B_g := Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g).
392	  % The conclusion is:
393	  %   ∃ (n : ℕ) (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
394	  %     (_ : Algebra (Localization.Away g) (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
395	  %     (φ : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g)
396	  %           →ₐ[Localization.Away g] Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)),
397	  %     Function.Injective φ ∧
398	  %     (letI := φ.toAlgebra;
399	  %      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) (Localization.Away g))
400	  %                    (Localization.Away (algebraMap A B g)))
401	  % The polynomial subalgebra A_g[b_1,…,b_n] is realised as the DOMAIN
402	  % `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`, embedded into B_g by the algebra map φ; the
403	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
404	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
405	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder
406	  % needed to type B_g as an A_g-algebra: the single φ then carries the algebra map,
407	  % `Function.Injective φ` encodes algebraic independence of the b_j, and
408	  % `Module.Finite … over φ.toAlgebra` encodes module-finiteness of B_g over the
409	  % image polynomial subalgebra. Downstream, L5 (\cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core})
410	  % consumes a finite module over `MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g`; the AlgHom hands it
411	  % exactly that algebra structure via `φ.toAlgebra`, with no instance revival.
412	  % Why B_g and not B: generic flatness only needs the property after one
413	  % inversion g; the integral-dependence equations only clear denominators after
414	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
415	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
416	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
418	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
419	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
420	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
421	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
422	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
423	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
424	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
425	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
426	  normalisation with denominators).}
427	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
428	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
429	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
430	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
431	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
432	  \[
433	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
434	  \]
435	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
436	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
437	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
438	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
439	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
444	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
445	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
446	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
447	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
448	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
449	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
450	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
451	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
452	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
453	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
454	
455	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
456	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
457	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
458	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
459	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
460	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
461	
462	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
463	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
464	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
465	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
466	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
467	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
468	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
469	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
470	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
471	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
472	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
473	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
474	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
475	
476	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
477	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
478	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
479	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
480	
481	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
482	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
483	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
484	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
485	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
486	  \[
487	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
488	  \]
489	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
490	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
491	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
492	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
493	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
494	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
495	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
496	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
497	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
498	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
499	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
500	
501	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
502	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
503	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
504	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
505	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
506	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
507	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
508	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
509	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
510	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
511	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
512	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
513	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
514	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
515	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
516	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
517	
518	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
519	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
520	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
521	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
522	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
523	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
524	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
525	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
526	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
527	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
528	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
529	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
530	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
531	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
532	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
533	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
534	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
535	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
536	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
537	\end{proof}

541	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
542	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
544	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
545	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
546	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:

548	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
549	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
550	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
551	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
552	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
553	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
554	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
555	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
556	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
557	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
558	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
559	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
560	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
561	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
562	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
563	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
564	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
565	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
566	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
567	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
568	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
569	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
570	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
571	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
572	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
573	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
574	  \[
575	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
576	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
577	  \]
578	
579	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
580	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
581	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
582	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
583	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
584	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
585	\end{lemma}
586	
587	\begin{proof}
588	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
589	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
590	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
591	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
592	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
593	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
594	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
595	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
596	
597	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
598	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
599	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
600	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
601	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
602	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
603	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
604	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
605	
606	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
607	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
608	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
609	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
610	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
611	\end{proof}

655	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)

663	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
664	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
665	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
666	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).

669	\begin{proof}
670	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
671	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
672	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
673	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
674	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
675	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
676	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
677	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
678	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
679	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
680	\end{proof}
